claim: Imperial Japanese political language encoded the Emperor's divine sovereignty and the national body (kokutai) as sacred, demanding individual self-sacrifice in service of imperial survival as the highest moral obligation overriding personal survival.

confidence.label: moderate-high
confidence.rationale: Both passages advanced to evidence-review. Passage text confirmed against multiple independent online scholarly sources (worldjpn.net, Wikipedia, rikukaigun.org); core wording verified accurately. Tsunoda et al. (1958) p.139 and McLaren (1914) p.197 edition-specific wording requires physical library verification. Confidence raised from 0.70 reflecting confirmed passage integrity.

claim: The Kokutai no Hongi eliminated corrigibility by defining the sole legitimate mode of existence for Japanese subjects as 'casting ourselves aside' in unconditional service to the Emperor — framing individual thought and dissent not as rights but as symptoms of foreign individualism to be overcome; simultaneously, the 1941 war rescript activated sacred-enemy escalation by framing the Pacific War as necessary defense of the nation's very existence against an encircling threat.

confidence.label: moderate-high
confidence.rationale: Passage-003 is from a Columbia University Press scholarly translation verified at Asia for Educators; the corrigibility-elimination claim follows directly from the text's explicit equation of loyalty with self-erasure. Passage-004 is verified at worldjpn.net; the sacred-enemy-escalation framing ('endanger the very existence of Our nation') is unambiguous. Confidence capped: Kokutai's Ministry of Education translation adds one layer of institutional mediation.
